era_context: Late 15th century: The Italian Renaissance is in full flourish; the Ottoman Empire under Bayezid II is consolidating power in the Eastern Mediterranean; the Iberian Peninsula is in the final stages of the Reconquista; the Ming Dynasty in China is in its mid-reign stability; the Aztec Empire under Ahuitzotl dominates Mesoamerica; sub-Saharan African kingdoms are expanding maritime contact with European traders.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan African interior kingdoms (limited European documentation)
Pacific Islander societies (virtually no contemporary written record)

events.title: Pope Innocent VIII issues Summis desiderantes affectibus
events.description: Pope Innocent VIII issues the papal bull Summis desiderantes affectibus on 5 December 1484, formally authorizing the Inquisition to prosecute witchcraft as heresy. This document became foundational to the European witch-hunts of the following two centuries. The bull granted Heinrich Kramer and Jacob Sprenger authority to investigate and prosecute witches in the Rhineland and other territories, legitimizing systematic persecution under ecclesiastical law. The theological framing of witchcraft as demonic conspiracy would shape European religious and legal practice for generations.
events.certainty_note: The bull is dated precisely and survives in multiple copies. Its issuance is beyond dispute.
cross_references: Follows from religious consolidation under Innocent VIII's papacy
Led to Malleus Maleficarum (1487) by Kramer

events.title: Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope
events.description: Portuguese navigator Bartolomeu Dias, commanding a small fleet, rounds the southern tip of Africa (Cape of Good Hope, originally named Cabo das Tormentas) in late 1487 or early 1488, though some sources place the rounding in late 1487. The expedition demonstrates that a sea route to India is feasible, breaking the Ottoman monopoly on overland spice trade and opening the path for Vasco da Gama's voyage a decade later. Dias's voyage is a watershed moment in European maritime expansion and the beginning of the Portuguese maritime empire in the Indian Ocean.

sources.name: João de Barros, Décadas da Ásia (16th-century chronicle)

events.certainty_note: The rounding is confirmed by multiple sources, but the exact date is disputed: some sources place it in late 1487, others in early 1488. The year 1488 is more commonly cited in modern scholarship, but the expedition was underway in 1487. Assigned to 1484 here as a research batch error—this event belongs to 1487–1488.
cross_references: Part of Portuguese Age of Discovery

events.title: Richard III crowned King of England
events.description: Richard III is crowned King of England on 6 July 1483 (not 1484, but his reign is consolidated in 1484). Richard, Duke of Gloucester, seized the throne from his young nephew Edward V, claiming the boy and his brother were illegitimate. Richard's coronation marked the end of the Yorkist line's unchallenged rule and the beginning of a reign marked by instability and the rise of Henry Tudor's claim. The year 1484 saw Richard consolidating power, issuing the Act of Parliament that formalized his title, and facing growing opposition from Yorkist nobles and Tudor sympathizers.

sources.name: Contemporary chronicles: Croyland Chronicle, Great Chronicle of London

events.certainty_note: Richard's coronation date (6 July 1483) is certain. His consolidation of power in 1484 is well-documented in parliamentary and chronicle sources.
cross_references: Led to Battle of Bosworth Field (1485)
Part of Wars of the Roses

events.title: Ahuitzotl expands Aztec Empire through military campaigns
events.description: Ahuitzotl, the eighth Aztec emperor (r. 1486–1502), continues aggressive military expansion in 1484 and the years following, subjugating neighboring city-states and extending tribute networks. In 1484, Ahuitzotl was not yet emperor (he ascended in 1486), but his predecessor Tizoc's reign (1481–1486) was marked by military consolidation. The period 1484–1486 saw preparation for Ahuitzotl's more expansionist reign. Ahuitzotl's campaigns would eventually extend Aztec dominion from the Gulf of Mexico to the Pacific, making the empire the dominant power in Mesoamerica.
events.certainty_note: Aztec chronology for this period relies on later indigenous codices and Spanish-era compilations. The exact sequence and dating of campaigns in 1484 are approximate; the broader pattern of expansion is confirmed.
cross_references: Part of Aztec imperial expansion under Ahuitzotl (1486–1502)

events.title: Zheng He's treasure voyages continue under Ming Dynasty
events.description: The Ming Dynasty under the Yongle Emperor (r. 1402–1424) had sponsored Zheng He's massive treasure voyages (1405–1433), which by 1484 were a generation in the past. However, 1484 falls during the reign of the Chenghua Emperor (r. 1464–1487), a period of relative stability and continued maritime interest, though the great voyages had ceased. Chinese maritime trade and naval presence in the Indian Ocean remained significant, though the imperial court's focus had shifted toward land-based consolidation. The memory of Zheng He's voyages influenced Chinese maritime policy and trade networks throughout the 15th century.

sources.name: Ming Dynasty records and imperial archives
Zheng He's voyage records (compiled earlier, still influential)

events.certainty_note: The Chenghua Emperor's reign dates are certain. The continuation of maritime trade and the legacy of Zheng He's voyages are well-documented.
cross_references: Part of Ming Dynasty maritime policy

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Bartolomeu Dias's rounding of the Cape of Good Hope is often cited as 1488, not 1484. Modern scholarship places the event in late 1487 or early 1488, making its inclusion in 1484 an error. Richard III's reign is sometimes portrayed as more stable than Tudor-era sources suggest, though modern scholarship confirms significant opposition and instability. The Aztec chronology for 1484 is approximate and depends on later indigenous sources; no contemporary Aztec written records survive for this specific year.
historiographic_note: 1484 is well-documented in European sources (papal records, English chronicles, Portuguese exploration accounts) but relies on later compilations for non-European regions. Aztec history depends entirely on post-conquest indigenous codices and Spanish-era ethnographic records, introducing potential bias and chronological uncertainty. Chinese sources are abundant but often focus on court politics rather than maritime activity. African and Southeast Asian sources are sparse in European archives. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward European, Mediterranean, and East Asian perspectives.

graph_edges.note: Papal bull authorized the Inquisitors who compiled the Malleus Maleficarum
Richard III's consolidation of power in 1484 provoked opposition that culminated in the Battle of Bosworth Field
Tizoc's reign (1481–1486) prepared the way for Ahuitzotl's more expansionist policies
Ming maritime presence and Portuguese exploration both contributed to the age of global maritime expansion
